content.text: When React calls your component function to produce a render, it passes the current state values as fixed constants — not live references. Every event handler, timeout callback, and expression created during that render "sees" exactly those values and nothing else. This behavior is described as state being a
: a photograph of the state at render time. Calling a state setter does not change the current snapshot — it schedules a new render with a new snapshot.

attrs.alt: Three consecutive renders each with their own frozen count value
attrs.title: Each render gets its own snapshot of state — event handlers close over that snapshot

content.text: Consider what happens when React renders a component. Your function runs. All the
calls return the current state values. Those values are captured as local

in that function call. Every event handler, inline callback, and child prop receives those constants. When the event handler runs later — possibly much later — it reads those captured constants, not whatever the state might be at that future moment.
three times does not produce three increments. All three calls read the same
constant (0), so React receives three requests to set state to
The snapshot model explains one of the most common sources of confusion in React: why callbacks that run later still see an "old" value.
This is not a bug — it is the intended behavior. The
function was created during a specific render, capturing
from that render's snapshot. Three seconds later, the
fires and reads the same captured value. The component may have re-rendered many times since then with a new
, but that does not affect the old closure.
Reading the Latest State in Async Code
When you need to read the
state value at the time a callback runs (not the snapshot when it was created), use a ref. Refs are mutable and are not part of the snapshot — they always reflect the latest assigned value.

Use a ref to escape the snapshot when you genuinely need the "current" value in an async callback. But be intentional: the snapshot behavior often protects you from reading inconsistent intermediate state. Think carefully about which value you actually want.

still returns the old value — the snapshot cannot change mid-render.
What "Setting State Triggers a Render" Really Means
Stores the new value in React's internal state store (not the current variable).
Schedules a re-render of the component.
returns the new value — producing a fresh snapshot.
is never mutated. It remains frozen at the value it had when the component function ran.

Every time your component renders, React creates new event handler functions. Each new function closes over the state values from that specific render. React attaches the new handlers to the DOM during the commit phase, replacing the old ones. This means clicking a button always calls the
version of the handler — the one from the latest render.
callback: setTimeout captures the handler at
time and does not get replaced. A click handler on the DOM
Snapshot vs Live: A Comparison Table
Multiple updates in one handler; updating in effects
Async callbacks that must see current state
Pitfall 1: Reading state after calling a setter
to compare old and new state. Mutating the existing object does not change the reference — React sees the same object and skips the re-render. Always produce a
object or array to replace state.
The snapshot model may feel surprising at first, but it provides strong guarantees:
: all of a render's output (JSX, event handlers, children) uses a single consistent set of state values. There is no race between reading different parts of state.
: given the same state snapshot, a pure component always produces the same output. This makes testing straightforward and behavior easy to reason about.

: in React 18 concurrent mode, a render might be interrupted and restarted. Because each render sees only its own frozen snapshot, restarting is safe — a partially rendered tree cannot have mutated the state.

A useful mental model: imagine React taking a photograph of your state before calling your component. Your component prints all its JSX and events using only what's in that photograph. When you call a setter, React schedules a new photograph. Until then, everyone in the current render is looking at the old photo.
Snapshot Semantics in Loops and Closures
Snapshot semantics surface most clearly in loops and delayed callbacks. Because each render captures a frozen
, any closure created in that render — whether a timeout, a promise handler, or an event listener — permanently closes over that render's value.

Click the button twice quickly. You might expect to see 0, 1, 2 — but instead each batch of three timeouts logs the count at the moment that click's render was snapped. The key insight: closures capture renders, not a live variable.
with a new snapshot. Timeouts from render #1 see count=0 forever. Timeouts from render #2 see count=1 forever. They can never see each other's snapshot.
Snapshot Model and Async / Concurrent React

The snapshot model becomes especially important with React's concurrent features. In concurrent mode, React may pause, discard, or restart a render. Because each render is a pure function of its own snapshot, partial renders can be thrown away safely — no mutations occurred.
When a component suspends, React can discard the half-rendered subtree and retry when data arrives. The snapshot ensures the retry starts from the same state.
Low-priority renders may be superseded by urgent ones. Snapshots guarantee each render is self-contained and throwing one away has no side effects on state.
Deferred renders run with older snapshot values intentionally — the snapshot model is what makes this safe and coherent.
Stale closures are the most common bug pattern that stems from snapshot semantics. Here is a systematic way to diagnose them:
A callback reads a state value that never changes even as the component re-renders.
The callback was created in an early render and never replaced — it's frozen to that render's snapshot.
(a) functional updater to avoid reading state, (b) add the value to effect deps so the closure refreshes, (c) use a ref to escape snapshot semantics.
are frozen constants for the duration of each render — they do not change mid-render.
Calling a state setter schedules a re-render with a new snapshot; it does not mutate the current snapshot.
Event handlers and async callbacks close over the state snapshot from the render in which they were created.
) to chain updates in a single event handler or to read the latest state inside an effect.
Use a ref mirroring the state when an async callback genuinely needs the latest value, not the snapshot.
Never mutate state objects — always produce a new object to ensure React detects the change and schedules a re-render.
